Probabilistic_Networks.graph: A graph is a pair G = (V, E), where V is a finite set of distinct nodes and E \subseteq V \times V is a set of links.
Probabilistic_Networks.directed link: An ordered pair (u, v)  \in E denotes a directed link from node u to node v, and u is said to be a parent of v and v a child of u.
Probabilistic_Networks.parent node: An ordered pair (u, v)  \in E denotes a directed link from node u to node v, and u is said to be a parent of v and v a child of u.
Probabilistic_Networks.child node: An ordered pair (u, v)  \in E denotes a directed link from node u to node v, and u is said to be a parent of v and v a child of u.
Probabilistic_Networks.the set of parents of a node: The set of parents of a node v shall be denoted by pa(v).
Probabilistic_Networks.the set of children of a node: The set of children of a node v shall be denoted by ch(v).
Probabilistic_Networks.n undirected link: If (u, v) \in E and (v, u) \in E, the link between u and v is an undirected link, denoted by {u, v} \in E.
Probabilistic_Networks.nodes being connected: Nodes u and v are said to be connected if (u, v) or (v, u) \in E.
Probabilistic_Networks.links meeting head to head at a node v: If (u, v) \in E and (w, v) \in E, then these links are said to meet head to head at v.
Probabilistic_Networks.directed graph: If E does not contain undirected links, then G is a directed graph.
Probabilistic_Networks.n undirected graph: If E does not contain directed links, then G is an undirected graph.
Probabilistic_Networks.path: A path <v_1, …, v_n> is a sequence of distinct nodes such that each consecuctive pair (v_i, v_{i+1}) is connected for each I = 1, …, n-1.
Probabilistic_Networks.length of a path: The length of a path is one less than the number of nodes in the path, which is also equal to the number of arcs in the path.
Probabilistic_Networks.directed path: A path is a directed path if each pair of connected nodes in the path is connected by a directed edge.
Probabilistic_Networks.n ancestor: If there is a directed path from v_i to v_j, then v_i is an ancestor of v_j.
Probabilistic_Networks.descendant: If there is a directed path from v_i to v_j, then v_j is a descendant of v_i.
Probabilistic_Networks.the set of ancestors of a node v: The notation for the set of ancestors of a node v is denoted an(v).
Probabilistic_Networks.the set of descendants of a node v: The notation for the set of descendants of a node v is de(v).
Probabilistic_Networks.set of non-descendants of a node v: The set nd(v) = V - de(v) \cup {v} is called the non-descendants of v.
Probabilistic_Networks.the set of non-descendants of v: The set nd(v) = V - de(v) \cup {v} is called the non-descendants of v.
Probabilistic_Networks.ancestral set of a set of vertices U: The ancestral set An(U) \subseteq V of a set U \subseteq V of a graph G = (V, E) is the set of nodes U \cup_{u \in U} an(u).
Probabilistic_Networks.the ancestral set of a set of nodes U: The ancestral set An(U) \subseteq V of a set U \subseteq V of a graph G = (V, E) is the set of nodes U \cup_{u \in U} an(u).
Probabilistic_Networks.path being blocked by a set: A path <v_1, …, v_n> from v_1 to v_n of an undirected graph, G = (V, E), is blocked by a set S \subseteq V if {v_2, …, _{n-1}} \cap S [neq] \emptyset.
Probabilistic_Networks.connected graph: A graph G = (V, E) is connected if for any pair {u, v} [subseteq] V there is a path <u, ..., v> in G.
Probabilistic_Networks.polytree: A connected graph G = (V, E) is a polytree (also known as a singly connected graph) if for any pair {u, v} \subseteq V there is a unique path <u, ..., v> in G.
Probabilistic_Networks.of (rooted) tree: A directed polytree with only a single orphan node is called a (rooted) tree.
Probabilistic_Networks.cycle: A cycle is a path <v_1, ..., v_n> of length greater than two with the exception that v_1 = v_n;
Probabilistic_Networks.directed cycle: A directed cycle is a cycle where all the edges are directed.
Probabilistic_Networks.directed acyclic graph: A directed graph with no directed cycles is called a directed acyclic graph, or simply a DAG.
Probabilistic_Networks.skeleton of a directed graph: The undirected graph obtained from a DAG, G, by replacign all its directed links with undirectedones is known as the skeleton of G.
Probabilistic_Networks.moral graph of a DAG: Let G = (V, E) be a DAG. The undirected graph G^m = (V, E^m), where E^m = {{u, v} | u and v are connected or have a common child in G}, is called the moral graph of G.           So G^m is obtained by first adding undirected links between pairs of unconnected nodes taht share a common child, and then replacing all directed links with undirected links.
Probabilistic_Networks.variable: A variable represents an exhaustive set of mutually exclusive events, referred to as the domain of the variable.
Probabilistic_Networks.domain of a variable: A variable represents an exhaustive set of mutually exclusive events, referred to as the domain of the variable.
Probabilistic_Networks.random variables and decision variables: Random variables represent random events and decision variables represent choices under the control of some, typically human, agent.
Probabilistic_Networks.three main classes of nodes in probabili: There are three main classes of nodes in probabilistic networks:      1. Nodes representing chance variables     2. Nodes representing decision variables     3. Nodes representing utility functions.
Probabilistic_Networks.evidence: Evidence is information received from external sources about the (possible) states/values of a subset of the variables of the network.
Probabilistic_Networks.evidence appear for a set of discrete ev: For a set of discrete evience variables, X, the evidence appears in the form of a likelihood distribution over the states of X; also called an evidence function or potential.
Probabilistic_Networks.n evidence function on a set of discrete: An evidence function, \mathcal{E}_X , for a set of discrete variables X is a function \mathcal{E}_X : dom(X) \to \mathbb{R}^+.
Probabilistic_Networks.evidence appear for a set of continuous: For a set of continuous evience variables, Y, the evidence appears in the form of a vector of real values, one for each variable in Y
Probabilistic_Networks.n evidence function on a set of continuo: An evidence function, \mathcal{E}_Y , for a set of continuous variables Y is a function \mathcal{E}_Y : Y \to \mathbb{R}.
Probabilistic_Networks.hard evidence: An evidence function that assigns zero probability to all but one state is said to provide hard evidence.
Probabilistic_Networks.soft evidence: An evidence function that does not provide hard evidence provides soft evidence.
Probabilistic_Networks.n instantiation of a variables X: Hard evidence on a variable X is often referred to as instantiation of X or that X has been observed.
Probabilistic_Networks.variable X being observed: Hard evidence on a variable X is often referred to as instantiation of X or that X has been observed.
Probabilistic_Networks.causal network: A causal network is a DAG where each link represents a causal mechanism.
Probabilistic_Networks.serial connection: A serial connection is a path of length three where the arcs point in the same direction.
Probabilistic_Networks.diverging connection: A diverging connection is a set of three connected nodes where the arcs flow from the middle node toward the two outer nodes.
Probabilistic_Networks.converging connection: A converging connection is a set of three connected nodes where the arcs flow into the middle node from the two outer nodes.
Probabilistic_Networks.flow of information in a serial connecti: In a serial connection, if we do not have hard evidence on the middle node, evidence about either of the end nodes will influence our belief in the other variables.

In a serial connection, if the state of the middle variable is known for sure through hard evidence, then the flow of information between the other two variables cannot take place through this connection.
Probabilistic_Networks.serial connections and the flow of info: Information may flow through a serial connection X \to Y \to Z unless the state of Y is known.
Probabilistic_Networks.flow of information in a diverging conne: In a diverging connection, if we do not have hard evidence on the middle node, evidence about either of the end nodes will influence our belief in the other variables.

In a diverging connection, if the state of the middle variable is known for sure through hard evidence, then the flow of information between the other two variables cannot take place through this connection.
Probabilistic_Networks.diverging connection and the flow of in: Information may flow through a diverging connection X \leftarrow Y \to Z unless the state of Y is known.
Probabilistic_Networks.flow of information in a converging conn: In a converging connection, if we do not have hard evidence on the middle node, then the flow of information between the other two variables cannot take place through this connection.

In a converging connection, if the state of the middle variable of a serial connection is known for sure through hard evidence, evidence about either of the end nodes will influence our belief in the other variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.converging connection and the flow of i: Information may flow through a converging connection X \to Y \leftarrow Z if evidence on Y or one of its descendents is available.
Probabilistic_Networks.explaining away: The property of converging connections, X \to Y \leftarrow Z, that information about the state of X (Z) provides an explanation for an observed effect on Y, and hence confirms or dismisses Z (X) as the cause of the effect, is often referred to as \"explaining away\" effect or \"intercausal inference\".
Probabilistic_Networks.intercausal inference: The property of converging connections, X \to Y \leftarrow Z, that information about the state of X (Z) provides an explanation for an observed effect on Y, and hence confirms or dismisses Z (X) as the cause of the effect, is often referred to as \"explaining away\" effect or \"intercausal inference\".
Probabilistic_Networks.variable being the direct cause of anot: A variable X is said to be a direct cause of Y if setting the value of X by force, the value of Y may change and there is no other variable Z that is a direct cause of Y such that X is a direct cause of Z.
Probabilistic_Networks.How are causal relationships represented: To correctly represent the dependence and independence relations that exist among a set of variables of a problem domain, it is very useful to have the causal relations among the variables be represented in terms of directed links from causes to effects.           That is, if X is a direct cause of Y, we should make sure to add a directed link from X to Y.
Probabilistic_Networks.the d-separation of two nodes u and v: If u \in V and v \in V are d-separated, then u [_|_] v. Similarly If V_1 \subseteq V and V_2 \subseteq V are d-separated in graph G then we say that U [_|_] V.
Probabilistic_Networks.u [_|_] v represent: If u \in V and v \in V are d-separated, then u [_|_] v.
Probabilistic_Networks.V_1 [_|_] V_2 represent: If V_1 \subseteq V and V_2 \subseteq V are d-separated in graph G then we say that V_1 [_|_] V_2.
Probabilistic_Networks.V_1 [_|_] V_2 | W represent: If V_1 \subseteq V and V_2 \subseteq V are d-separated given hard evidence on W then we say that V_1 [_|_] V_2 | W.
Probabilistic_Networks.V_1 [not _|_] V_2 | W represent: If V_1 \subseteq V and V_2 \subseteq V are d-connected given hard evidence on W then we say that V_1 [not _|_] V_2 | W.
Probabilistic_Networks.X [_||_] Y represent: If X and Y are marginally independent then we say that X [_||_] Y.
Probabilistic_Networks.X [_||_] Y | Z represent: If X and Y are conditionally independent given hard evidence on Z, then we say that X [_||_] Y | Z.
Probabilistic_Networks.X [not _||_] Y | Z represent: If X and Y are conditionally dependent given hard evidence on Z, then we say that X [not _||_] Y | Z.
Probabilistic_Networks.d-separated elements and symmetry: d-separation relations are always symmetric, i.e. u [_|_] v implies that v [_|_] u.
Probabilistic_Networks.d-connected elements and symmetry: d-connection relations are always symmetric, i.e. u [not _|_] v implies that v [not _|_] u.
Probabilistic_Networks.independence and symmetry: Independence relations are always symmetric, i.e. X_U [_||_] X_v implies that X_v [_||_] X_U.
Probabilistic_Networks.dependence and symmetry: Dependence relations are always symmetric, i.e. X_U [not _||_] X_v implies that X_v [not _||_] X_U.
Probabilistic_Networks.d-separation rule: A path \pi = <u, ..., v> in a DAG, G = (V, E) is said to be blocked by S \subseteq V if \pi contains a node w such that either:      w \in S and the links of \pi do not meet head-to-head at w, or     w \not\in S, de(w) \cap S \ne \emptyset,a nd the links of \pi meet head-to-head at w.
Probabilistic_Networks.a path to be blocked by a set S: A path \pi = <u, ..., v> in a DAG, G = (V, E) is said to be blocked by S \subseteq V if \pi contains a node w such that either:      w \in S and the links of \pi do not meet head-to-head at w, or     w \not\in S, de(w) \cap S \ne \emptyset,a nd the links of \pi meet head-to-head at w.
Probabilistic_Networks.sets of vertices being d-separated: For three (not necessarily disjoint) subsets A, B, S of V, A and B are said to be d-separated if all paths between A and B are blocked by S.
Probabilistic_Networks.Directed Global Markov Property: Let G = (V, E) be a DAG and A, B, S be disjoint sets of V. Then each pair of nodes (\alpha \in A, \beta \in B) are d-separated by S whenever each path from \alpha to \beta is blocked by nodes in S in the graph (G_{An(A \cup B \cup S)})^m          This says taht A [_|_]_G B | S if all paths from A to B pass at least one node in S in the moral graph f the sub-DAG induced by the ancestral set of A \cup B \cup S.
Probabilistic_Networks.propability of an event: The chance that an element sampled randomly from the universe U belongs to X defines the probability that the element belongs to X, and is denoted P(X).
Probabilistic_Networks.the proability of an event: The chance that an element sampled randomly from the universe U belongs to X defines the probability that the element belongs to X, and is denoted P(X).
Probabilistic_Networks.probability of the union of two events: The probability that a random element from the universe U belongs to X \cup Y is defined as P(X \cup Y) = P(X) + P(Y) - P(X \cap Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.conditional probability of X given Y: The conditional probability of X given Y, denoted P(X | Y)  is defined as P(X \cup Y) / P(Y) = P(X, Y) / P(Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.n event: In general, an event can be considered as an outcome of an experiment, a particular observarion of a value of a variable (or set of variables), etc.
Probabilistic_Networks.probability distribution: As a probabilistic network, define a probability distribution over a set of variables V as an event x \in dom(X) (i.e. a vector of values) of a subset of variables X \subseteq V.
Probabilistic_Networks.axioms of Bayesian probability: The following three axioms provide the basis for Bayesian probability calculus:      Axiom 1. For any event a, 0 \le P(a) \le 1, with P(a) = 1 if and only if a occurs with certainty.      Axiom 2. For any two mutually exclusive events a and b the probability that either a or b occur is P(a or b) = P(a) + P(b).      In general, if events a_1, ..., a_n are pairwise exclusive, then P(\cup_{i = 1 to n} a_i) = P(a_1) + ... + P(a_n) = \Sigma_{i = 1 to n} P(a_i).           Axiom 3. For any two events a and b the probability that both a and b occur is      P(a and b) = P(a, b) = P(a|b)P(b).           Axiom 1 simply says taht a probability is a nonnegative real number less than or equal to 1, and that it equals 1 if and only if the associated event has happened for sure.      Axiom 2 says that if two events cannot co-occur, then the probability that either one of them occurs equals the sum of the probabilities of their individual occurrences.      Axiom 3 is sometimes referredto as the fundamental rule of probability calculus. The axiom says that the probability of the co-occurrence of two events a and b can be computed as the product of the probability of event a (b) occurring conditional on the fact that b (a) has already occurred and the probability of event b (a) occurring.
Probabilistic_Networks.rule of total probability: Let P(X, Y) be a joint probability distribution for two variables X and Y with dom(X) = {x_1, …, x_m} and dom(Y) = {y_1, …, y_n}. Using the fact that dom(X) and dom(Y) are exhaustive sets of mutually exclusive states of X and Y, respectively, Axiom 2 of probability gives us the rule of total probability:      \foralli: P(x_i) = P(x_{i, y_{1) + ... + P(x_i, y_n) = \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_i, y_j).           This means that we can calculate P(X) from P(X, Y) as      P(X) = (\Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_1,y_j), ..., \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_m, y_j))     This is equivalent to P(X) = \Sigma_YP(X, Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.marginalizing out Y of P(X, Y): Let P(X, Y) be a joint probability distribution for two variables X and Y with dom(X) = {x_1, ..., x_m} and dom(Y) = {y_1, ..., y_n}. Using the fact that dom(X) and dom(Y) are exhaustive sets of mutually exclusive states of X and Y, respectively, Axiom 2 of probability gives us the rule of total probability:      \foralli: P(x_i) = P(x_i, y_1) + ... + P(x_i, y_n) = \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_i, y_j).           This means that we can calculate P(X) from P(X, Y) as      P(X) = (\Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_1,y_j), ..., \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_m, y_j))     This is equivalent to P(X) = \Sigma_YP(X, Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.eliminating Y from P(X, Y): Let P(X, Y) be a joint probability distribution for two variables X and Y with dom(X) = {x_1, ..., x_m} and dom(Y) = {y_1, .., y_n}. Using the fact that dom(X) and dom(Y) are exhaustive sets of mutually exclusive states of X and Y, respectively, Axiom 2 of probability gives us the rule of total probability:      \foralli: P(x_i) = P(x_i, y_1) + ... + P(x_i, y_n) = \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_i, y_j).           This means that we can calculate P(X) from P(X, Y) as      P(X) = (\Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_1,y_j), ..., \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} P(x_m, y_j))     This is equivalent to P(X) = \Sigma_YP(X, Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.probability potential: A probability potential is a nonnegative function defined over the product space over the domains of a set of variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.How is a probability potential turned in: A probability potential is turned into a probability distribution through the operation of normalization.
Probabilistic_Networks.normalization of a probability potentia:
A (probability) potential, \phi_X defined on dom(X), is turned into a probability distribution, P(X), through the operation known as normalization. We shall use the notation 
u(\phi_X) to denote normalization of \phi_X, where 
u(\phi_X) is defined as      
u(\phi_X) = \phi_X / \Sigma_X \phi_X.
Probabilistic_Networks.conditional normalization: The conditional probability distribution P(X | Y) can be obtained from the joint distribution P(X, Y) through conditional normalization with respect to X:      \mu_X(P(X, Y)) = P(X, Y) / \Sigma_YP(X, Y) = P(X, Y) / P(X) = P(Y | X).
Probabilistic_Networks.normalization of a conditional probabili: \mu_Y(P(X | Y)) = P(X | Y) since \Sigma_X P(X | Y) = 1_Y.
Probabilistic_Networks.vacuous potential: A uniform potential e.g. 1_Y is called a vacuous potential.
Probabilistic_Networks.prior probability distribution: Before any evidence has been taken into account the probability distribution P(X') for a subset X' \subseteq X of variables is referred to as the prior probability distribution for X'.
Probabilistic_Networks.posterior probability distribution: The conditional probability distribution P(X' | \epsilon), where X' \subseteq X and \epsilon denotes evidence, is referred to as the posterior probability distribution for X' given \epsilon.
Probabilistic_Networks.posterior joint distribution: Given an evidence potential \epsilon_{E} on a subset E \subseteq X (expressing \epsilon, the posterior joint distribution is obtained as P(X', \epsilon) = \Sigma_{X \ X'} P(X) * \epsilon_{E}.
Probabilistic_Networks.posterior conditional distribution: Given an evidence potential \epsilon_{E} on a subset E \subseteq X (expressing \epsilon, the posterior conditional distribution is achieved through normalization of the posterior joint distribution:      P(X' | \epsilon) = \mu(P(X', \epsilon)).
Probabilistic_Networks.multiplication of potentials: Let \phi and \psi be potentials defined on dom(X) and dom(Y) respectively, and let z \in dom(X \cup Y) be an arbitrary element (configuration). We define \phi * \psi as      (\phi * \psi(z)= \phi(z_X)\psi(z_Y), where z_X and z_Y are projections of z to dom(X) and dom(Y), respectively.
Probabilistic_Networks.ddition of potentials: Let \phi and \psi be potentials defined on dom(X) and dom(Y) respectively, and let z \in dom(X \cup Y) be an arbitrary element (configuration). We define \phi + \psi as      (\phi + \psi(z)= \phi(z_X)+ \psi(z_Y), where z_X and z_Y are projections of z to dom(X) and dom(Y), respectively.
Probabilistic_Networks.division of potentials: Let \phi and \psi be potentials defined on dom(X) and dom(Y) respectively, and let z \in dom(X \cup Y) be an arbitrary element (configuration). We define \phi / \psi as      (\phi / \psi(z)= 0 if \phi(z_X = 0, \phi(z_X) / \psi(z_Y) if \phi(z_Y [neq] 0, undefined otherwise,      where z_X and z_Y are projections of z to dom(X) and dom(Y), respectively.
Probabilistic_Networks.distributive law for potentials: Let \psi and \phi be potentials defined on dom(X) = {x_1, …, x_m} and dom(Y) = {y_1, …, y_n}, where X \cap Y = \emptyset. Using the distributive law, we then get      \Sigma_{X \ X'}\Sigma_{Y\Y'}(\phi*\psi) = \Sigma_{X \ X'} \phi \sigma_{Y \ Y'} \psi,      where X' \subseteq X amd Y' \subseteq Y and \Sigma_X\phi \Sigma_Y\psi is short for \Sigma_X(\phi * \Sigma_Y \psi)).
Probabilistic_Networks.barren variable: Variables of a graphical model that have no observed descendants, are never observed themselves, and for which we do not wish to compute their posterior probabilities are called barren variables, as they provide no relevant information to the inference process. In fact they provide \"information\" in the form of vacuous potentials and hence may be removed from the model.
Probabilistic_Networks.fundamental rule of probability calculu: The fundamental rule of probability calculus is      P(X, Y) = P(X | Y) P(Y) = P(Y | X) P(X).
Probabilistic_Networks.What is Bayes' rule: Bayes' rule says that P(Y | X) = P(X | Y) P(Y) / P(X). Because the denominator is constant for all states of Y, we often write Bayes' rule as P(Y | X) [proportional to] P(X | Y) P(Y), which means that P(Y | X) is proportional to P(X | Y) P(Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.likelihood distribution: The quantity P(x | Y) = L(Y | x) is called the likelihood of Y given x.
Probabilistic_Networks.What is Bayes's rule in terms of prior,: In general, Bayes' rule states that posterior is proportional to prior times likelihood.
Probabilistic_Networks.graphical interpretation of Bayes' rule: Application of Bayes' rule can also be given a graphical interpretation. Consider two variables A and B and a model P(A, B) = P(A)P(B | A). To apply Bayes' rule on this model we perform the following calculations:      (i)P(A, B) = P(A)P(B | A)     (ii)P(B) = \Sigma_AP(A, B), and     (iii)P(A | B) = P(A, B)/P(B). Thus, one way of interpreting the application of bayes' rule is through so called arc-reversal.
Probabilistic_Networks.arc-reversal application of Bayes' rule: Application of Bayes' rule can also be given a graphical interpretation. Consider two variables A and B and a model P(A, B) = P(A)P(B | A). To apply Bayes' rule on this model we perform the following calculations:      (i)P(A, B) = P(A)P(B | A)     (ii)P(B) = \Sigma_AP(A, B), and     (iii)P(A | B) = P(A, B)/P(B). Thus, one way of interpreting the application of bayes' rule is through so called arc-reversal.
Probabilistic_Networks.Bayes' factor: The notion of Bayes' factor is usefl to calculate the relative support provided by an observation, Y = y, for two competing hypotheses: H_0: X = x_0 and H_1 : X = x_1. The factor B is defined as the posterior odds ratio over the prior odds ratio, which can be represented as      (P(x_0 | y) / P(x_1 | y)) / (P(x_0) / P(x_1))      = L(x_0 | y) / L(x_1 | y)
Probabilistic_Networks.Bayesian likelihood ratio: The notion of Bayesian likelihood ratio is usefl to calculate the relative support provided by an observation, Y = y, for two competing hypotheses: H_0: X = x_0 and H_1 : X = x_1. The factor B is defined as the posterior odds ratio over the prior odds ratio, which can be represented as      (P(x_0 | y) / P(x_1 | y)) / (P(x_0) / P(x_1))      = L(x_0 | y) / L(x_1 | y)
Probabilistic_Networks.the value of the Bayes factor B and two: If B is the Bayes factor of the competing hypotheses H_0 : X = x_0 and H_1 : X = x_1, where B = L(x_0 | y) / L(x_1 | y), then      B > 1 if the observation provides more support for H_0 than H_1     B < 1 if the observation provides less support for H_0 than for H_1, and      B = 1 if the observation does not provide useful information for differentiating between H_0 and H_1.
Probabilistic_Networks.variable being independent of another: A variable X is independent of another variable Y with respect to a probability distribution P if P(x | y) = P(x) for all x \in dom(X) and all y \in dom(Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.two variables being independent: The notations for two variables being independent is X [_||_] Y.
Probabilistic_Networks.marginal independence of two variables: A variable X is marginally independent of another variable Y with respect to a probability distribution P if P(x | y) = P(x) for all x \in dom(X) and all y \in dom(Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.marginal independence and the joint dis: The joint ditribution of marginally independent variables is equal to the product of the marginal distribitions. i.e.if X [_||_] Y, then     P(X, Y) = P(X)P(Y).
Probabilistic_Networks.variables being conditionally independe: A variable X is conditionally independent of Y given Z (with respect to a probability distribution P) if      P(x | y, z) = P(x | z) for all x \in dom(X), all y \in dom(Y), and all z \in dom(Z).
Probabilistic_Networks.conditional independence: If X and Y are conditionally independent given Z, then it is denoted as X [_||_] Y | Z in our standard notation.
Probabilistic_Networks.How does Bayes' rule apply conditional i: From Bayes' rule P(X, y, Z) = P(X | Z) P(Y | Z) P(Z).
Probabilistic_Networks.dependence map of a probability distrib: Let P be a probability distribution over a set of variables X_v and let G = (V, E) be a DAG. Then for each A, B, S \subseteq V, where A \cap B = \emptyset, G is a dependence map (or D-map) of P if      X_A [_||_]_P X_B | X_S implies A [_|_]_G B | S.
Probabilistic_Networks.n independence map of a probability dist: Let P be a probability distribution over a set of variables X_v and let G = (V, E) be a DAG. Then for each A, B, S \subseteq V, where A \cap B = \emptyset, G is an independence map (or D-map) of P if      A [_|_]_G B | S implies X_A [_||_]_P X_B | X_S.
Probabilistic_Networks.perfect map of a probability distributi: Let P be a probability distribution over a set of variables X_v and let G = (V, E) be a DAG. Then for each A, B, S \subseteq V, where A \cap B = \emptyset, G is a perfect map of P if it is both a D-map and an I-map:     X_A [_||_]_P X_B | X_S if and only if A [_|_]_G B | S.
Probabilistic_Networks.DAGs and perfect maps: DAGs defined in terms of recursive factorizations of joint probability distributions will always be perfect maps, unless there are some regularities in the probability distributions that entails additional independences.
Probabilistic_Networks.topological ordering of the variables o: A topological ordering of the variables v_1, …, v_n is one such that pa(v_i) \subseteq {v_1, …, v_{i-1}} for all I = 1, …, n          This means that the parents of each variable are selected before the variable itself.
Probabilistic_Networks.chain rule in a DAG: Because a DAG is an I-map, node conditional independence implies variable conditional independence. So the joint distribution can be simplified to P(X_{v_1}, …, X_{v_n}) = \Pi_{i = 1 to n} P(X_{v_i} | X_{pa(v_i)}).
Probabilistic_Networks.Bayesian network: A (discrete) Bayesian network, N = (X, G, P) over the variables, X, consists of      a directed acyclic graph G = (V, E) with nodes V = {v_1, ..., v_n} and directed links E.      a set of discrete random variables, X, represented by the nodes of G.      A set of conditional probability distributions, P, containing one distribution, P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}), for each random variable X_v \in X
Probabilistic_Networks.conditioning variables of X_v: In the conditional probability distribution P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) \in P, the set of variables represented by the parents, pa(v), of v \in V in G = (V, E) are sometimes called the conditioning variables of X_v.
Probabilistic_Networks.conditioned variable: In the conditional probability distribution P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) \in P,  X_v is called the conditioned variable.
Probabilistic_Networks.What does it mean to solve a Bayesian ne: To solve a Bayesian network N = (X, G, P) is to compute all posterior marginals given a set of evidence \epsilon, i.e. P(X|\epsilon) for all X \in X. If the set of evidence is empty, i.e. \epsilon = \emptyset, then the task is to compute all prior marginals, i.e. P(X) for all X \in X.
Probabilistic_Networks.the family of a node: The family of a node v is represented by fa(v).
Probabilistic_Networks.family of a node: The family of a node v is fa(v) = pa(v) \cup {v}.
Probabilistic_Networks.How is the complexity of a Bayesian netw: The complexity of a Bayesian network is defined in terms of the family fa(v) with the largest state space size ||X_{fa(v)}|| = |dom(X_{fa(v)})|.
Probabilistic_Networks.the state space size of a family and th: The state space size of a family grows exponentially with the size of the family.
Probabilistic_Networks.What is a secondary measure of the compl: A secondary measure of the complexity of a Bayesian network is the number of cycles and the length of cycles in its graph.
Probabilistic_Networks.minimal Bayesian network: A Bayesian network N = (X, G, P) is minimal if and only if, for every variable X_v \in X and for every parent Y \in X_{pa(v)}, X_v is not independent of Y given X_{pa(v)} \ {Y}.           This says that the parent set X_{pa(v)} of X_v should be limited to the set of variables with a direct impact on X_v.
Probabilistic_Networks.linear conditional Gaussian (LCG) Bayes: A linear conditional Gaussian (LCG) Bayesian network N = (X, G, P, F) consists of      A directed acyclic graph G = (V, E) with nodes V and directed links E.      A set of random variable X, represented by the nodes of G.      A set of conditional probability distributions P, containing one distribution P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}), for each discrete random variable X_v.      A set of conditional linear Gaussian probability density functions F, containing one density function, p(Y_v | X_{pa(v)}), for each continuous random variable Y_v.
Probabilistic_Networks.continuous random variable having a lin: A continuous random variable, X, has a linear conditional Gaussian distribution (or LCG distribution), conditional on the configuration of the parent variables (Z \subseteq X_{}, I \subseteq X_{\delta} if L(X | Z = z, I = i) = \mathbb{N}(A(i) + B(i)^{T}z, C(i)), where A is a table of mean values (one for each configuration i of the discrete parent variables I), B is a table of regression coefficient vectors (one vector for each configuration i of I with one regression coefficient for each continuous parent variable), and C is a table of variances (one for each configuration i of I). Notice that the mean value A(i) + B(i)^{T}z of X depends linearly on the values of the continuous parent variables Z, while the variance is independent of Z.
Probabilistic_Networks.joint distribution over all variables in: The joint distribution over all the variables in an LCG Bayesian network has the form P(X_{\Delta} = i) * [normals]_{|X_{\gamma}|(\mu(i), \sigma^{2}(i)), where [normals]_k(\mu, \sigma^{2}) denotes a k-dimensional Gaussian distribution .
Probabilistic_Networks.How does the chain rule of Bayesian netw: The chain rule of LCG Bayesian networks is     P(X_{\Delta} = i) * [Normals]_{|X_{\gamma}(\mu(i), \sigma^{2}(i)) = \Pi_{v \in V_{\Delta} P(i_v | I_{pa(v)}) * \Pi_{w \in V_{\gamma} P(y_w | X_{pa(w)}), for each configuration i of X_{\Delta}.
Probabilistic_Networks.influence diagrams and Bayesian network: An influence diagram can be considered as a Bayesian network augmented with decision variables, utility functions specifying the preferences of the decision maker, and a precedence ordering.
Probabilistic_Networks.utility: As decision makers, we are interested in making the best possible decisions given our model of the problem domain (the influence diagram). We assocuate utilities with state configurations of the network.
Probabilistic_Networks.utility function: Different utilities are represented by utility functions. Each utility function associates a utility value with each configuration of its domain variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.value function: Different utilities are represented by value functions. Each value function associates a utility value with each configuration of its domain variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.objective of decision analysis: The objective of decision analysis is to identify the decision options that produce the highest expected utility.
Probabilistic_Networks.procedure for identifying the decision w: To identify the decision opion with the highest expected utility, we compute the expected utility of each decision alternative.           If A is a decision variable with options a_1, ..., a_m, H is a hypothesis with states h_1, ..., h_n, and \epsilon is a set of observations in the form of evidence, then we can compute the utility of each outcome of the hypothesis and the expected utility of each action. The utility of an outcome (a_i, h_j) is U(a_i, h_j), where U(.) is our utility function.           The expected utility of performing action a_i is      EU(a_i) = [sum]_j U(a_i, h_j) P(h_j | \epsilon),      where P(.) represents our beliefs in H given \epsilon. The utility function U(.) encodes teh preferences of the decision maker on a numerical scale.
Probabilistic_Networks.formula for the expected utility of perf: The expected utility of performing action a_i is      EU(a_i) = [sum]_j U(a_i, h_j) P(h_j | \epsilon),      where P(.) represents our beliefs in H given \epsilon. The utility function U(.) encodes teh preferences of the decision maker on a numerical scale.
Probabilistic_Networks.maximum expected utility principle: The maximum expected utility principle states that we choose the alternative with the highest expected utility.           This amounts to selecting an option a* such that      a* = arg max_{a \in A} EU (a).
Probabilistic_Networks.observarions and actions: There is an important difference between observations and actions. An observation of an event is passive in the sense that we assume that an observation does not effect the state of the world whereas the decision of an action is active in the sense that an action enforces a certain event.
Probabilistic_Networks.n intervening action: The event enforced by a decision may or may not be included in the model depending on whether or not the event is relevant for the reasoning. If the event enforced by an action A is represented in our model, then A is referred to as an intervening action.
Probabilistic_Networks.non-intervening action: The event enforced by a decision may or may not be included in the model depending on whether or not the event is relevant for the reasoning. If the event enforced by an action A is not represented in our model, then A is referred to as a non-intervening action.
Probabilistic_Networks.discrete influence diagram: A (discrete) influence diagram N = (X, G, P, U) consists of       - A DAG G = (V, E) with nodes, V, and directed links, E, encoding dependence relations and information precedence including a total order on decisions.       - A set of discrete random variables, X_C and discrete decision variables, X_D, such that X = X_C \cup X_D represented by nodesof G.       - A set of conditional probability distributions, P, containing one distribution, P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) for each discrete random variable X_v.       - A set of utility functions, U, containing one utility function, u(X_{pa(v)}), for each node v in the subset V_U \subseteq V of utility nodes.
Probabilistic_Networks.no-forgetting assumption: The no-forgetting assumption says that perfect recall is assumed of all observations and decisions made in the past.
Probabilistic_Networks.influence diagrams and the no forgettin: An influence diagram supports the representation and solution of sequential decision problems with multiple local utility functions under the no-forgetting assumption.
Probabilistic_Networks.How should influence diagrams be constru: An influence diagram, N = (X, G, P, U), should be constructed such that one can determine exactly which vaiables are known prior to making each decision.      An observarion X_v made prior to a decision D_w is represented in N by makign v a parent of w in G.      If v is a parent of w in G = (V, E), then it is assumed that X_v is observed prior to making the decision represented by D_w. The link (v, w) id then referred to as an informational link.
Probabilistic_Networks.n informational link: An observarion X_v made prior to a decision D_w is represented in N by makign v a parent of w in G.      If v is a parent of w in G = (V, E), then it is assumed that X_v is observed prior to making the decision represented by D_w. The link (v, w) id then referred to as an informational link.
Probabilistic_Networks.How is the total order on the decision v: In an influence diagram, there must be a total order on the decision variables X_D = {D_1, ..., D_n} \subseteq X. So there can only be one sequence in which the decisions are made. We add informational links to specify a total order (D_1, ..., D_n) on X_D = {D_1, ..., D_n}
Probabilistic_Networks.What do the different links in an influe: A link (w, v) into a node representing a random variable X_v denotes a possible probabilistic dependence relation of X_v on Y_w while a link from a node representing a variable X into a node representing a decision variable D denotes taht the state of X is known when the decision D is to be made. A link (w, v) into a node representing a utility function u denotes functional dependence of u on X_v \in X.
Probabilistic_Networks.chain rule of influence diagrams: The chain rule of influence diagrams is      EU(X) = \Pi_{X_v \in X_{C}} P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) \Sigma_{w \in V_{U}} U(X_{pa(w)}).
Probabilistic_Networks.the total order on decisions and all th: As a consequence of the total order on decisions and the set of informational links, the set of discrete random variables and decision variables are subjected to a partial ordering. The random variables are partitioned into disjoint information sets I_0, ..., I_n relative to the decision variables sepecifying the precedece order. The partition induces a partial ordering \prec on the variables X. The set of variables observed between decisions D_i and D_{i+1} precedes D_{i+1} and succeeds D_i in the ordering as follows:      I_0 \prec D_i \prec I_1 \prec ... \prec D_n \prec I_n,      where I_0 is the set of discrete random variables observed before the first decision, I_i is the set of discrete random variables observed after making decision D_i and before makign decision D_{i+1}, for all i = 1, ..., n-1, and i_n is the set of discrete random variables never observed or observed after the last decision D_n has been made.
Probabilistic_Networks.What does it mean to solve an influence: To solve an influence diagram N = (X, G, P, U) with decision variables X_D is to identify an optimal strategy \hat{Delta} over X_D maximizing the expected utility for the decision maker and to compute the maximum expected utiluty MEU(\hat{Delta}) of \hat{Delta}.
Probabilistic_Networks.strategy: A strategy \Delta is an ordered set of decision policies \Delta  = (\delta_1, ..., \delta_n) including one decision policy for each decision D \in X_D.
Probabilistic_Networks.n optimal strategy: An optimal strategy \hat{Delta} = (\hat{Delta}_1, ..., \hat{Delta}_n) maximizes the expected utility over all possible strategies, i.e. it satisfies      EU(\hat{Delta}) \ge EU(\Delta) for all other strategies \Delta.
Probabilistic_Networks.decision history of a decision: The decision history of D_i, denoted H(D_i) is the set of previous decisions and their parent variables      H(D_i) = \cup_{j = 1 to i-1}({D_j} \cup X_{pa(v_j)}) = {D_1, ..., D_{j-1}} \cup \bigcup_{j = 0 to i-1} I_j, where v_j is denoting the node that represents D_j.
Probabilistic_Networks.decision past of D_j: The decision past of D_i, denoted I(D_i) is the set of its parent variables and the decision history of H(D_i)     I(D_i) = X_{pa(v_i)} \cupH(D_i) = {D_1, …, D_{i-1}} \cup \bigcup_{j = 1 to i-1}I_j.
Probabilistic_Networks.decision future of a decision: The decision future of D_i, denoted F(D_i) is the set of its decendant variables     F(D_i) = {D_{i+1}, ..., D_n} \cup \bigcup_{j = i to n}I_j.
Probabilistic_Networks.policy: A policy \delta_i is a mapping from the information set I(D_i) of D_i to the state space dom(D_i) of D_i such that \delta_i: I(D_i) \to dom(D_i).      A policy for decision D specifies the optimal action for each decision maker for all possible observations made prior to making decision D.
Probabilistic_Networks.n intervening decision: An intervening decision D of an influence diagram is a decision that may impact the value of another variable X represented in the model.
Probabilistic_Networks.linear-quadratic conditional Gaussian i: A linear -quadratic conditional Gaussian (LQCG) influence diagram N = (X, G, P, F, U) consists of     A DAG G = (V, E) with nodes V and directed links E encoding dependence relations and information precedence including a total order on decisions.      A set of random variables X_C and decision variables X_D such that X = X_C \cup X_D represented by nodes of G.     A set of conditional probability distributions P containing one distribution P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) for each discrete random variable X_v.      A set of linear-conditional Gaussian probability density functions F containing one density function, p(Y_w | X_{pa(w)}) for each continuous random variable Y_w     A set of linear-quadratic utility functions, U, containing one utility function u(X_{pa(v)} for each node v in the subset V_U \subseteq V of utility nodes.
Probabilistic_Networks.chain rule for linear-quadratic conditio: The chain rule for LQCG influence diagrams is      EU(X_{\Delta} = I, X_{\gamma}) = P(X_{\Delta = i}) * \mathbb{N}_{|X_{\gamma}(\mu(i), \sigma^{2}(i)) * [Sum]_{z \in V_U u(X_{pa(z)}) for each configuration i of X_{\Delta}.
Probabilistic_Networks.limited memory influence diagram: A limited memory influence diagram (LIMID) N = (X, G, P, U) consists of      A DAG G = (V, E) with nodes V and directed links E encoding depende relations and information precedence.      A set of random variables X_C, and discrete decision variables X_D such that X = X_C \cup X_D represented by nodes of G.      A set of conditional probability distributions P containing one distribution P(X_v | X_{pa(v)}) for each discrete random variable X_v.      A set of utility functions U containing one utility function u(X_{pa(v)}) for each node v in the subset V_U \subseteq V of utility nodes.
Probabilistic_Networks.Which problems can be modeled with LIMID: All decision problems that can be represented as ordinary influence diagrams can also be represented as a LIMID.
Probabilistic_Networks.object orientation: Object orientation may be defined in the following way     object-orientation = objects + inheritance, where objects are instances of classes and inheritance defines a relationship between classes.
Probabilistic_Networks.basic variable in an object oriented pr: In an OOPN, a node that does not represent an instance of a network class is said to represent a basic variable.
Probabilistic_Networks.n instance\/: An instance represents an instantiation of a network class within another network class
Probabilistic_Networks.network class: A network class C is a DAG over three pairwise disjoing sets of nodes I(C), H(C), O(C) where I(C) are the input nodes H(C) are the hidden nodes and O(C) are the output nodes of C.
Probabilistic_Networks.n object oriented probabilistic network: An object oriented probabilistic network (OOPN) network class C = (N, I, O) consitst of      A probabilistic network N over variables X with DAG G.      A set of basic variables I \subseteq X specified as input variables and a set of basic variables O \subseteq X specified as output variables such that I \cap O = \emptyset and H = X \ (I \cup O).
Probabilistic_Networks.graphical representation of instances of: In the graphical representation of an OOPN instances are represented as rectangles with arc shaped corners.
Probabilistic_Networks.How does an OOPN implement information h: An OOPN implements information hiding through encapsulation. If X is a variable of a network instance N, then X is the simple name of the variable, whereas N.X is the qualified name of the variable.
Probabilistic_Networks.simple name of a variable: The simple name of a variable is the variable name itself, without reference to the network instance it is a member of.
Probabilistic_Networks.qualified name of a variable: The qualified name of a variable is the network instance of the variable \"dot\" the variable neme.
Probabilistic_Networks.long name of a variable: The long  name of a variable is the network instance of the variable \"dot\" the variable neme.
Probabilistic_Networks.scope of a variable: The scope of a variable X [mathbb S](X) of a variable X is defined as the part of a model in which the declaration of X can be referred to by its simple name.
Probabilistic_Networks.scope of a network class: The (internal) scope [mathbb S](C) of a network class C is the set of variables and instances which can be refrred to by their simple names inside C.
Probabilistic_Networks.bound variable: An input variable I of an instance of network class \mathcal{C} is bound if it has a parent X in the network class encapsulating .
Probabilistic_Networks.binding link: A link into a node representing an input variable may be referred to as a binding link.
Probabilistic_Networks.variables being equivalent: Two variables X and Y are equivalent if and only if they are of the same kind, category, and subtype with the same state labels in the case of discrete variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.chain rule for OOPNs: The chain rule for OOPNs says that P(\mathcal{X}) = P(X) \Pi_{Y_v \not\in X} P(Y_v | X_{pa(v)}).
Probabilistic_Networks.n unfolded OOPN: The unfolded network model of an object oriented network \mathcal{N} is obtained by recursively unflding the instance nodes of \mathcal{N}.
Probabilistic_Networks.flat OOPN: The flat network model of an object oriented network \mathcal{N} is obtained by recursively unflding the instance nodes of \mathcal{N}.
Probabilistic_Networks.the joint distribution of an object ori: The joint distribution of an object oriented Bayesian network model is equivalent to the joint distribution of its unfolded network model.
Probabilistic_Networks.instance tree of an object oriented mod: The instance tree T of an object oriented model \mathcal{N} is a tree over the set of instances of classes in \mathcal{N}. Two nodes v_i and v_j in T are connected by an undirected link if and only if the instance represented by v_i contains the instance represented by v_j. The root of an instance tree is the top level network class not instantiated in any other network class within the model.
Probabilistic_Networks.inheritance: We define inheritance as the ability of an instance to take its interface definition from another instance.
Probabilistic_Networks.most critical problem related to the eff: The most critical problem related to the efficiency of the inference process in a Bayesian network is that of finding the optimal order in which to perform the computations.
Probabilistic_Networks.query against a Bayesian network: We define the notion of a query Q against a Bayesian network model \mathcal{N} as follows:      Let \mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{P}) be a Bayesian network model. A query Q is a three-tuple Q = (\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{T}, \epsilon), where \mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{X} is the target set and \epsilon is the evidence set.
Probabilistic_Networks.solution of a query: The solution of a query Q is the posterior distribution over the target, i.e. P(\mathcal{T} | \epsilon).
Probabilistic_Networks.target variable: A variable X is a target variable if X \in \mathcal{T}.
Probabilistic_Networks.barren variable with respect to queries: Let \mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{P}) be a Bayesian network and let Q = (\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{X}, \epsilon) be a query against \mathcal{N}. A variable X is a barren variable with respect to Q, if X \not\in \mathcal{T}, X \not\in \epsilon, and all descendents de(X), of X are barren.
Probabilistic_Networks.nuisance variable with respect to a que: Let \mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{P}) be a Bayesian network and let Q = (\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{X}, \epsilon) be a query against \mathcal{N}. A non-barren variable X is a nuisance variable with respect to Q, if X \not\in \mathcal{T}, X \not\in \epsilon, and X is not on the path between any pair of variables Y \in \mathcal{T} and Z \in \epsilon.           A nuisance variable is computationally relevant for the query Q, but is not on a path between any pair of evidence and query variables.
Probabilistic_Networks.relevant network of a query: The relevant network of a query consists of target variables, evidence variables, and variables on paths between target and evidence variables only.
Probabilistic_Networks.procedure for the arc reversal process: Let \mathcal{G} be the DAG of a Bayesian network \mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{P}) and assume a query Q = ([script] N, {Z}, \emptyset) against \mathcal{N}. The inference task of arc reversal is to compute P(Z) by eliminating all variables \mathcal{X} \ {Z}          The inference on the arc reversal process on \mathcal{G} has a natural graphical interpretation as a sequence f arc reversals and barren variable eliminations. The fundamental idea is to adjust the structure of \mathcal{G} such that all variables except Z are pruned as barren variables while maintaining the underlying properties of the joint probability distributions over the remaining variables. The structure of \mathcal{G} is adjusted through a sequence of arc reversals options.      Assume X_w is the next variable to be eliminated as a barren variable. Let X_w have parents X_{pa(w)} = X_i \cup x_j and X_v have parents X_{pa(v)} = {X_w} \cup X_j \cup X_k where X_i \cap X_j = X_i \cap X_k = X_j \cap X_k = \emptyset such that X_i = X_{pa(w)} \ X_{pa(v)} are the parents specific for X_w, X_j = X_{pa(w)} \cap X_{pa(v)} are the common parents, and X_k = X_{pa(v)} \ X_{pa(w)} are the parents specific for X_v.      The reversal of arc (w, v) proceeds by setting X_{pa(w)} = X_i \cup X_j \cup X_k \cup {X_v} and X_{pa(v)} = X_i \cup X_j \cup X_k as well as performing the following computations:      P(X_v | X_i, X_j, X_k) = \Sigma_{X_w} P(X_w | X_i, X_j) P(X_v | X_w, X_j, X_k)     P(X_w | X_v, X_i, X_j, X_k) = P(X_w | X_i, X_j) P(X_v | X_w, X_j, X_k) / P(X_v | X_i, X_j, X_k)     The operation of reversing an arc changes the structure of \mathcal{G} without changing the underlying joint probability distribution over \mathcal{X} induced by \mathcal{N}.      Once the arc (w, v) has been reversed, the variable X_w is a barren variable relative to the other variables and can be eliminated from \mathcal{G} without further computations.           The basic idea of the inference process known as arc reversal is to perform a sequence of arc reversals and barren variable eliminations on the DAG \mathcal{G} untils a desired marginal or conditional is obtained. In this process a valid Bayesian network structure is maintained thro
Probabilistic_Networks.junction tree representation of a Bayes: The junction tree representation \mathcal{T} of a Bayesian network \mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{P}) is a pair \mathcal{T} = (\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{S}), where \mathcal{C} is the set of cliques and \mathcal{S} is the set of separators. The cliques \mathcal{C} are the nodes of \mathcal{T} whereas the separator \mathcal{S} annotate the links of the tree. Each clique C \in \mathcal{C} represents a maximal complete subset of the pairwise connected variables of \mathcal{X}, i.e. C \subseteq \mathcal{X}, of an undirected graph.
